name: Transfer Agent Agreement
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Transfer Agent Agreement establishing the relationship between a company and its transfer agent for managing shareholder records, stock transfers, and securities compliance. Incorporates SEC requirements under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and industry best practices tailored to the issuer's context, such as public status and transfer restrictions. Use this skill when appointing or renewing a transfer agent for efficient shareholder administration and corporate governance.
content:
# Transfer Agent Agreement Drafting Workflow

You are a specialized corporate securities attorney tasked with drafting a comprehensive Transfer Agent Agreement. This critical corporate document establishes the legal relationship between a company (the issuer) and its appointed transfer agent for managing shareholder records and stock transfers. Your draft must comply with applicable securities regulations, including SEC requirements under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, and reflect current industry best practices for corporate governance and shareholder administration.

## Understanding the Document's Purpose and Context

A Transfer Agent Agreement is a specialized securities document that governs the appointment and duties of a transfer agent—the entity responsible for maintaining accurate shareholder records, processing stock transfers, issuing and canceling certificates, and ensuring compliance with securities laws. Before beginning your draft, gather essential information about the specific transaction. Search any uploaded documents to identify the parties involved, the classes of securities to be serviced, any existing transfer agent relationships being terminated, and specific service requirements unique to this issuer. Look for corporate resolutions authorizing the engagement, board minutes discussing the appointment, or correspondence outlining negotiated terms. If the issuer has previously used transfer agent services, review those agreements to understand what worked well and what provisions need modification.

The agreement must be precise, comprehensive, and aligned with both federal securities regulations and state corporate law. Consider the issuer's specific characteristics: Is this a publicly traded company subject to full SEC reporting requirements, or an issuer relying on exemptions? What is the size and complexity of the shareholder base? Are there transfer restrictions, such as Rule 144 legends or contractual lock-ups, that the transfer agent must monitor and enforce? Understanding these contextual factors will enable you to tailor the agreement appropriately while maintaining essential protections for both parties.

## Drafting the Parties, Recitals, and Effective Date

Begin by clearly identifying all parties to the agreement with complete legal precision. For the issuer, provide the full corporate name exactly as it appears in the certificate of incorporation, the state of incorporation, the principal place of business address, and identify the specific corporate officer authorized to execute the agreement on behalf of the company. Verify this information by searching uploaded corporate documents, including certificates of good standing, corporate resolutions, or organizational documents. For the transfer agent, include the complete legal entity name, SEC registration status as a transfer agent (including the SEC registration number, which typically begins with "84-"), the principal business address, and the authorized signatory information. Confirm the transfer agent's current registration status by referencing their Form TA-1 or Form TA-2 filings if available in your documents.

Establish the effective date of the agreement with precision, specifying whether it is the date of execution, the date of final signature, or a different commencement date tied to a specific event such as the termination of a prior transfer agent relationship. Include substantive recitals that provide business context and establish the foundation for the agreement. These recitals should explain the issuer's need for transfer agent services, identify the specific classes or series of securities to be serviced (such as "Common Stock, par value $0.001 per share" or "Series A Preferred Stock"), reference any relevant corporate actions authorizing the appointment, and acknowledge the transfer agent's qualifications and SEC registration status. Well-drafted recitals serve both as interpretive aids and as representations that can be relied upon by both parties.

## Defining the Appointment and Comprehensive Scope of Services

Draft a comprehensive appointment clause that formally designates the transfer agent as the agent for the specified securities. Clearly state whether the appointment is exclusive or non-exclusive, as this affects the issuer's ability to engage additional transfer agents for the same securities. Specify the exact classes and series of securities covered by the agreement, using the precise designations from the issuer's certificate of incorporation. Address whether the appointment extends automatically to future issuances of the same class or series, or whether amendments will be required for new securities.

Delineate the complete scope of services with specificity that leaves no ambiguity about the transfer agent's responsibilities. The core services should include maintaining the official shareholder registry and stock ledger in compliance with state corporate law requirements, processing transfers of securities and updating ownership records upon receipt of proper transfer documentation, issuing physical stock certificates or maintaining book-entry positions in accordance with issuer instructions, and handling requests for replacement certificates due to loss, theft, destruction, or mutilation. Specify the transfer agent's obligations regarding dividend and distribution processing if this service is included, detailing whether the transfer agent will serve as paying agent or merely provide shareholder data to a separate paying agent.

Address the transfer agent's compliance obligations comprehensively. The transfer agent must maintain compliance with SEC rules governing transfer agents, particularly Rules 17Ad-1 through 17Ad-20, which establish requirements for registration, recordkeeping, safeguarding of funds and securities, and operational capabilities. Specify the transfer agent's obligations regarding the preparation and filing of regulatory reports, including any required submissions to the issuer's state of incorporation. Detail the procedures for handling transfer restrictions, including the transfer agent's duty to refuse transfers that would violate securities laws or contractual restrictions, the process for removing restrictive legends upon receipt of appropriate legal opinions or issuer instructions, and the maintenance of stop transfer lists.

Include detailed provisions for handling special corporate events and transactions. Specify the transfer agent's role and responsibilities in connection with stock splits and reverse stock splits, including the mechanics of exchanging old certificates for new ones and adjusting shareholder records. Address name changes, requiring the transfer agent to issue new certificates reflecting the new corporate name and to update all records and systems accordingly. Cover corporate reorganizations, mergers, and acquisitions, establishing the transfer agent's obligations to process exchanges of securities and to coordinate with successor entities or their transfer agents. Clarify the transfer agent's authority to rely on written instructions from designated issuer representatives, and establish clear protocols for authenticating transfer requests, verifying the legitimacy of transactions, and obtaining necessary supporting documentation such as medallion signature guarantees, legal opinions, or court orders.

## Establishing Compensation, Fees, and Expense Reimbursement

Establish a clear and detailed compensation structure that eliminates ambiguity about all fees payable to the transfer agent. Begin with the base fee structure, which may include an annual retainer fee for maintaining the shareholder registry regardless of activity levels, or a monthly fee that provides more frequent billing cycles. Specify whether the base fee covers a certain number of shareholder accounts or transactions, with additional per-account or per-transaction fees applying above those thresholds. Detail all transaction-based fees with precision, including per-transfer fees for processing ownership changes, per-certificate fees for issuing physical stock certificates, fees for processing certificate replacements or cancellations, and fees for handling escheatment of unclaimed property.

Address setup and onboarding fees if applicable, including any one-time charges for establishing the shareholder registry, converting records from a prior transfer agent, or implementing customized procedures or systems. If the fee structure includes volume-based pricing tiers or discounts that apply when transaction volumes exceed certain thresholds, specify these clearly with the applicable breakpoints and reduced rates. Establish the billing cycle, whether monthly, quarterly, or annually, and specify payment terms such as "net 30 days from invoice date." Identify acceptable payment methods, such as wire transfer, ACH, or check, and provide the necessary payment instructions.

Detail the reimbursement of reasonable out-of-pocket expenses incurred by the transfer agent in performing its duties. Specify categories of reimbursable expenses, which typically include postage and courier services for mailing certificates and shareholder communications, fees charged by outside counsel for reviewing legal opinions submitted in connection with legend removal or transfer requests, costs associated with extraordinary regulatory compliance requirements or special projects requested by the issuer, and expenses related to responding to legal process such as subpoenas or court orders. Establish any limitations on expense reimbursement, such as requiring prior issuer approval for expenses exceeding a specified threshold, or excluding certain categories of expenses from reimbursement.

Include provisions addressing fee adjustments over the term of the agreement. Specify whether fees are subject to annual increases, and if so, whether these increases are tied to a specific index such as the Consumer Price Index or are subject to mutual agreement between the parties. Establish the notice period required for fee changes, typically requiring 60 to 90 days' advance written notice. Address the issuer's obligation to continue paying fees even if services are suspended due to the issuer's failure to provide necessary information, failure to pay invoices when due, or other issuer defaults. If applicable, specify any termination fees or charges for transitioning records to a successor transfer agent, including fees for providing shareholder data in specific formats or for extended transition support.

## Defining Term, Renewal, and Comprehensive Termination Provisions

Define the initial term of the agreement with specific start and end dates, such as "a period of three years commencing on the Effective Date and ending on [specific date]," or specify that the agreement continues on a month-to-month or year-to-year basis until terminated by either party in accordance with the agreement's termination provisions. If the agreement includes automatic renewal provisions, clearly state the renewal term length (such as successive one-year periods) and the notice period required to prevent automatic renewal (typically 60 to 90 days before the end of the then-current term).

Establish comprehensive termination rights that provide both parties with appropriate flexibility while ensuring continuity of essential shareholder services. Provide for termination for convenience, allowing either party to terminate the agreement without cause upon providing written notice at least 60 to 90 days in advance, which gives both parties adequate time to arrange for transition of services. Include termination for material breach, allowing the non-breaching party to terminate if the other party fails to cure a material breach within a specified cure period, typically 30 days after receiving written notice detailing the breach. Specify particular breaches that may justify immediate termination without a cure period, such as the transfer agent's loss of SEC registration, the issuer's failure to pay undisputed fees for more than a specified period (such as 60 days past due), or either party's material violation of confidentiality obligations.

Address termination rights triggered by extraordinary events, including either party's insolvency, bankruptcy, appointment of a receiver, or assignment for the benefit of creditors, which may justify immediate termination given the fundamental impact on the party's ability to perform. Include force majeure termination rights, allowing either party to terminate if a force majeure event prevents performance for an extended period, such as 90 consecutive days. Specify the transfer agent's obligation to terminate if it ceases to be registered with the SEC as a transfer agent, as continued performance would violate securities laws.

Detail the comprehensive obligations of both parties upon termination to ensure orderly transition and continuity of shareholder services. The transfer agent must cooperate fully in transitioning all records and services to a successor transfer agent designated by the issuer, providing complete and accurate shareholder data in formats reasonably requested by the issuer or successor agent, including electronic files compatible with standard transfer agent systems. Specify the transfer agent's obligation to return all issuer property, including stock certificates, corporate seals, signature stamps, and any other materials provided by the issuer. Require the transfer agent to return or certify destruction of all confidential information in accordance with the agreement's confidentiality provisions, subject to any retention required by law or regulation.

Address the issuer's obligations upon termination, including the duty to pay all fees and expenses incurred through the effective date of termination, to reimburse all reasonable costs associated with the transition to a successor agent, and to provide the transfer agent with appropriate releases and confirmations once the transition is complete. Include provisions requiring the transfer agent to continue providing essential services during a reasonable transition period, typically 30 to 60 days, to ensure that shareholder transactions can be processed without interruption and that the successor agent can be properly onboarded. Specify that the transfer agent's obligations to maintain confidentiality, provide indemnification for pre-termination acts, and cooperate with the issuer survive termination of the agreement.

## Crafting Representations, Warranties, and Ongoing Covenants

Include mutual representations and warranties that establish the foundation of the parties' relationship and provide assurances about their legal status and authority. The issuer should represent and warrant that it is a corporation duly organized, validly existing, and in good standing under the laws of its state of incorporation, with full corporate power and authority to enter into and perform its obligations under the agreement. The issuer should represent that the agreement has been duly authorized by all necessary corporate action, including board approval and any required shareholder consent, and that the execution, delivery, and performance of the agreement do not violate the issuer's organizational documents, any applicable law or regulation, or any agreement to which the issuer is a party or by which it is bound.

The issuer should make specific representations regarding the securities subject to the agreement, warranting that all such securities are duly authorized and validly issued in accordance with applicable corporate law and securities regulations, and that the issuer has provided the transfer agent with accurate and complete information regarding the authorized capitalization, outstanding securities, and any transfer restrictions or special rights applicable to the securities. Include the issuer's representation that it will provide accurate and complete information necessary for the transfer agent to perform its duties, including timely notice of corporate actions, changes in authorized shares, or modifications to transfer restrictions.

The transfer agent should represent and warrant that it is duly registered with the SEC as a transfer agent pursuant to Section 17A of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, providing its SEC registration number and confirming that such registration is in full force and effect without any pending proceedings that could result in suspension or revocation. The transfer agent should represent that it maintains adequate systems, procedures, internal controls, and technology infrastructure to perform the services in compliance with applicable laws and regulations, including the SEC's operational capability requirements under Rule 17Ad-4. Include representations that the transfer agent maintains adequate insurance coverage, including errors and omissions insurance and fidelity bond coverage, in amounts consistent with industry standards for transfer agents servicing similar issuers.

The transfer agent should represent that it has the necessary expertise, resources, and qualified personnel to fulfill its obligations under the agreement, and that it will comply with all applicable federal and state securities laws and regulations in performing the services. Include representations regarding the transfer agent's compliance with data security and privacy requirements, warranting that it maintains appropriate administrative, technical, and physical safeguards to protect shareholder information from unauthorized access, use, or disclosure.

Include ongoing covenants that impose continuing obligations on both parties throughout the term of the agreement. Both parties should covenant to maintain their legal existence and good standing in their respective jurisdictions of organization, to comply with all applicable laws and regulations in performing their obligations under the agreement, and to maintain all necessary licenses, registrations, and permits required to perform their obligations. Require both parties to maintain appropriate insurance coverage and to provide evidence of such coverage upon request. Include covenants requiring prompt notification of material events, such as changes in control, loss of necessary registrations or licenses, material litigation or regulatory proceedings, or any events that could materially impair the party's ability to perform its obligations under the agreement.

## Allocating Risk Through Indemnification, Liability Limitations, and Insurance

Draft comprehensive indemnification provisions that appropriately allocate risk between the parties based on their respective areas of control and responsibility. The issuer should indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the transfer agent, its affiliates, and their respective officers, directors, employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, losses, liabilities, damages, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs) arising from or relating to the issuer's breach of any representation, warranty, covenant, or obligation under the agreement. The issuer's indemnification should cover claims arising from inaccurate, incomplete, or misleading information provided by the issuer to the transfer agent, the transfer agent's good faith reliance on instructions, certifications, or opinions provided by or on behalf of the issuer, and the validity, authorization, or legal status of the securities themselves, including claims that securities were not duly authorized or validly issued.

The issuer should indemnify the transfer agent for claims arising from the issuer's violation of securities laws or other applicable laws, except to the extent such violations result from the transfer agent's own misconduct. Include indemnification for claims by shareholders or other third parties arising from the issuer's corporate actions, dividend policies, or business decisions, as opposed to the transfer agent's processing errors. Specify that the issuer's indemnification obligations apply even if the claim is based on the transfer agent's strict liability or on legal theories that do not require proof of fault, provided the claim arises from matters within the issuer's area of responsibility.

The transfer agent should indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the issuer, its affiliates, and their respective officers, directors, employees, and agents from and against claims arising from the transfer agent's negligence, willful misconduct, or bad faith in performing services under the agreement. The transfer agent's indemnification should cover claims arising from the transfer agent's breach of representations, warranties, covenants, or obligations under the agreement, unauthorized actions taken without proper issuer authorization or contrary to issuer instructions, and violations of securities laws or regulations in performing services, including failures to comply with SEC rules governing transfer agent operations.

Include indemnification for claims arising from the transfer agent's failure to maintain adequate security safeguards, resulting in unauthorized access to or disclosure of shareholder information or other confidential information. Specify that the transfer agent's indemnification obligations cover claims by shareholders arising from the transfer agent's errors in processing transactions, maintaining records, or issuing certificates, except to the extent such errors result from the issuer's provision of inaccurate information or instructions.

Establish reasonable limitations on liability that protect both parties from disproportionate exposure while preserving meaningful remedies for material breaches. Include a provision excluding consequential, indirect, incidental, special, or punitive damages, except in cases of gross negligence, willful misconduct, fraud, or breach of confidentiality obligations. Consider including a liability cap that limits each party's aggregate liability under the agreement to a specified multiple of the annual fees paid or payable under the agreement, such as two or three times the annual fees, with exceptions for indemnification obligations, confidentiality breaches, and claims arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct.

Specify that indemnification obligations do not apply to claims arising from the indemnified party's own gross negligence, willful misconduct, bad faith, or material breach of the agreement. Include detailed procedural provisions governing indemnification claims, requiring the indemnified party to provide prompt written notice of any claim to the indemnifying party, allowing the indemnifying party to assume control of the defense and settlement of the claim (subject to the indemnified party's right to participate with its own counsel at its own expense), and requiring the indemnified party to cooperate reasonably in the defense. Specify that the indemnifying party's obligation to indemnify is conditioned on the indemnified party not admitting liability or settling the claim without the indemnifying party's prior written consent.

Require the transfer agent to maintain errors and omissions insurance with coverage limits appropriate for the size and nature of the issuer's shareholder base, typically ranging from $1 million to $10 million or more for larger issuers. Require the transfer agent to maintain a fidelity bond covering employee dishonesty and other specified risks in an amount consistent with industry standards and regulatory requirements. Specify that the issuer should be named as an additional insured or loss payee on applicable policies where appropriate, and require the transfer agent to provide certificates of insurance evidencing the required coverage upon request and to provide advance notice of any cancellation or material reduction in coverage.

## Protecting Confidential Information and Shareholder Data

Establish robust confidentiality obligations that protect sensitive shareholder information and proprietary business information exchanged between the parties. Define "Confidential Information" comprehensively to include all non-public information disclosed by either party to the other, whether orally, in writing, or in electronic form, including shareholder data and records, trading and transaction information, corporate records and documents, business strategies and plans, financial information, and any other information that is marked as confidential or that a reasonable person would understand to be confidential given the nature of the information and the circumstances of disclosure.

Require both parties to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information using the same degree of care used to protect their own confidential information of similar nature, but in no event less than reasonable care. Specify that Confidential Information may be used solely for purposes of performing obligations and exercising rights under the agreement, and may not be used for any other purpose without the prior written consent of the disclosing party. Require both parties to implement reasonable administrative, technical, and physical security measures to prevent unauthorized access, use, or disclosure of Confidential Information, and to limit access to employees, agents, and professional advisors who have a legitimate need to know the information and who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as protective as those in the agreement.

Specify permitted disclosures that do not violate the confidentiality obligations, including disclosures required by applicable law, regulation, court order, or other legal process, provided that the disclosing party provides advance written notice to the other party where legally permissible to enable the other party to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy. Include exceptions for disclosures to professional advisors, including attorneys, accountants, and auditors, who are bound by professional confidentiality obligations. Clarify that information is not considered Confidential Information if it was already known to the receiving party without confidentiality restrictions prior to disclosure, is or becomes publicly available through no breach of the agreement by the receiving party, is independently developed by the receiving party without use of or reference to the Confidential Information, or is rightfully received from a third party without confidentiality restrictions.

Address the transfer agent's specific obligations regarding shareholder data and personally identifiable information. Require the transfer agent to comply with all applicable federal and state privacy laws and regulations, including state data breach notification laws that may require notification to affected individuals and regulatory authorities in the event of unauthorized access to personal information. Specify the transfer agent's obligations under regulations such as the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act if applicable, requiring the implementation of comprehensive information security programs and the provision of privacy notices to shareholders.

Include detailed provisions addressing cybersecurity and data protection. Require the transfer agent to maintain information security programs that include industry-standard security protocols such as encryption of data in transit and at rest, multi-factor authentication for system access, regular security assessments and penetration testing, employee training on data security and privacy, and incident response plans for addressing security breaches. Require the transfer agent to promptly notify the issuer of any data breach, security incident, or unauthorized access affecting shareholder information or other Confidential Information, providing detailed information about the nature and scope of the incident, the data affected, and the remedial measures being implemented.

Specify the obligations of both parties upon termination of the agreement regarding Confidential Information. Require the return or certified destruction of all Confidential Information, including all copies and derivatives, within a specified period after termination, subject to any retention required by applicable law or regulation. Clarify that the confidentiality obligations survive termination of the agreement and continue for a specified period, typically three to five years after termination, or indefinitely for information that constitutes trade secrets under applicable law.

## Establishing Governing Law, Jurisdiction, and Dispute Resolution

Specify the governing law that will apply to the interpretation, construction, and enforcement of the agreement. Select the law of a state that has a well-developed body of corporate and commercial law, typically the state where the issuer is incorporated (which provides consistency with the law governing the issuer's internal affairs) or the state where the transfer agent maintains its principal place of business (which may be more familiar to the transfer agent). Include a clear statement that the agreement is governed by the specified state law without regard to its conflicts of law principles, which prevents the application of another state's law through conflict of law rules.

Establish the exclusive jurisdiction and venue for any legal proceedings arising out of or relating to the agreement. Designate either the state and federal courts located in a specific jurisdiction, such as the state and county where the issuer maintains its principal place of business or where the transfer agent is located, or alternatively, provide for binding arbitration as the primary dispute resolution mechanism. If selecting litigation in courts, include a consent to jurisdiction clause in which both parties irrevocably submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the designated courts and waive any objection to venue or inconvenient forum. Include a waiver of jury trial if the parties wish to have any disputes resolved by a judge rather than a jury, which can provide more predictable outcomes in complex commercial disputes.

If arbitration is selected as the dispute resolution mechanism, specify the arbitration rules that will govern the proceeding, such as the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association or JAMS Comprehensive Arbitration Rules & Procedures. Specify the number of arbitrators (typically one for smaller disputes or three for larger, more complex disputes), the seat or location of the arbitration, and the procedures for selecting arbitrators, including any qualifications required such as experience in securities law or corporate matters. Address whether the arbitration decision is binding and final with limited grounds for appeal, or whether any appeal rights exist under the applicable arbitration rules. Specify how arbitration costs and fees will be allocated, whether in accordance with the arbitration rules or based on the outcome of the dispute.

Include a provision requiring the parties to attempt good faith negotiation before initiating formal legal proceedings or arbitration. Specify that either party may initiate the negotiation process by providing written notice to the other party describing the dispute, and require senior executives of both parties to meet within a specified period (such as 15 or 30 days) to attempt to resolve the dispute. Consider including a mediation requirement, specifying that if negotiation does not resolve the dispute within a specified period, the parties will submit the dispute to non-binding mediation before a mutually acceptable mediator before proceeding to litigation or arbitration.

Specify that the prevailing party in any dispute is entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees, expert witness fees, and costs incurred in connection with the dispute, which provides an incentive for parties to assess the merits of their positions carefully and can deter frivolous claims. Ensure that the dispute resolution provisions do not prevent either party from seeking equitable relief, such as temporary restraining orders or preliminary injunctions, in a court of competent jurisdiction when necessary to prevent immediate and irreparable harm, particularly for breaches of confidentiality obligations or unauthorized use of proprietary information where monetary damages may be inadequate.

## Incorporating Essential General Provisions

Include comprehensive miscellaneous provisions that govern the administration, interpretation, and enforcement of the agreement. Address amendments and modifications by requiring that any changes to the agreement be made in writing and signed by authorized representatives of both parties, which prevents claims of oral modifications and ensures that all amendments are properly documented and approved. Establish detailed notice requirements, specifying that all notices, requests, demands, and other communications required or permitted under the agreement must be in writing and delivered by specified methods such as personal delivery, nationally recognized overnight courier service, certified or registered mail with return receipt requested, or email to designated addresses with confirmation of receipt.

Specify when notices are deemed received, such as upon personal delivery, one business day after deposit with an overnight courier, three business days after deposit in the mail, or upon confirmation of receipt for email delivery. Require each party to designate a specific address and email address for notices and to promptly notify the other party of any changes to such contact information. Include a severability clause providing that if any provision of the agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, such provision will be modified to the minimum extent necessary to make it valid and enforceable, or if such modification is not possible, the provision will be severed from the agreement, and the remaining provisions will continue in full force and effect.

Add an entire agreement clause stating that the agreement, together with any exhibits, schedules, and attachments, constitutes the complete and exclusive understanding between the parties concerning the subject matter and supersedes all prior negotiations, understandings, agreements, and representations, whether written or oral. This provision prevents either party from relying on prior statements or agreements that are not incorporated into the final written agreement. Include provisions addressing assignment, typically prohibiting either party from assigning the agreement or any rights or obligations under it without the prior written consent of the other party, with exceptions for assignments to affiliates or to successors in connection with a merger, consolidation, or sale of all or substantially all assets, provided the assignee assumes all obligations under the agreement.

Include a waiver provision stating that no failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power, or privilege under the agreement operates as a waiver of such right, and no single or partial exercise of any right precludes any other or further exercise of that right or any other right. Specify that any waiver must be in writing and signed by the party granting the waiver. Add a counterparts provision allowing the agreement to be executed in multiple counterparts, each of which is deemed an original and all of which together constitute one and the same instrument, and specify that electronic signatures and PDF or other electronic copies of signatures have the same legal effect as original signatures.

Include a force majeure provision excusing either party from liability for failure or delay in performance caused by events beyond its reasonable control, such as acts of God, war, terrorism, civil unrest, labor disputes, government actions, pandemics, or failures of telecommunications or internet infrastructure, provided the affected party provides prompt notice to the other party and uses commercially reasonable efforts to resume performance as soon as practicable. Specify which obligations are not excused by force majeure, such as payment obligations. Add a survival provision specifying which provisions survive termination or expiration of the agreement, including indemnification obligations, confidentiality obligations, payment obligations for services rendered prior to termination, dispute resolution provisions, and any other provisions that by their nature should survive.

Consider including provisions requiring compliance with applicable anti-corruption laws, including the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act if applicable, prohibiting bribery, kickbacks, and other corrupt practices in connection with the agreement. Include compliance with economic sanctions and export control regulations administered by the Office of Foreign Assets Control and other agencies, requiring both parties to represent that they are not subject to sanctions and will not engage in transactions that violate sanctions laws. Address anti-money laundering compliance, particularly relevant for transfer agents who may be subject to Bank Secrecy Act requirements, requiring the transfer agent to maintain appropriate AML policies and procedures.

Add any industry-specific provisions relevant to the transfer agent relationship and the regulatory framework governing transfer agents. Reference compliance with SEC Rule 17Ad-1 through 17Ad-20, which establish comprehensive requirements for transfer agent registration, recordkeeping, safeguarding of funds and securities, operational capabilities, and other aspects of transfer agent operations. If the issuer is subject to specific regulatory requirements, such as those applicable to registered investment companies or business development companies, include provisions requiring the transfer agent to comply with such requirements in performing services.

## Finalizing the Agreement with Professional Standards

Throughout the drafting process, maintain rigorous consistency in defined terms, ensuring that capitalized terms are used consistently throughout the agreement and that all defined terms are actually used in the operative provisions. Verify all cross-references to other sections, exhibits, or schedules to ensure accuracy. Use precise legal language appropriate for a securities-related agreement while ensuring that the document remains clear and readable for business professionals who will be implementing and administering the agreement.

Verify that all regulatory references are current and accurate, particularly SEC rules and regulations governing transfer agents, as these rules are periodically amended and updated. Search available resources to confirm current rule numbers and requirements. Ensure that the agreement reflects current industry practices and standards for transfer agent services by considering recent developments in transfer agent operations, technology, and regulatory compliance.

Consider the specific circumstances of this particular issuer and transaction. Review any uploaded documents to identify unique requirements, such as specific transfer restrictions that must be enforced, unusual classes or series of securities with special rights or features, or particular service level requirements based on the issuer's shareholder base characteristics. Tailor the agreement to address these specific needs while maintaining the essential protections and standard provisions that should be included in every transfer agent agreement.

Review the completed draft for internal consistency, ensuring that all provisions work together coherently and that there are no contradictions or gaps in coverage. Verify completeness by confirming that all essential topics have been addressed and that the agreement provides clear answers to foreseeable questions about the parties' rights and obligations. Ensure compliance with applicable law by reviewing the agreement against relevant securities regulations, state corporate law requirements, and any other applicable legal requirements.

Prepare all exhibits and schedules referenced in the agreement, ensuring they are properly formatted and contain all necessary information. Common exhibits include detailed fee schedules showing all applicable fees and charges, service level agreements specifying performance standards and metrics, forms of notices to be used for various purposes under the agreement, and specimen certificates or other forms to be used by the transfer agent. Ensure that all exhibits are properly referenced in the body of the agreement and that the agreement clearly states which party is responsible for preparing or providing each exhibit.

The final document should be a comprehensive, enforceable agreement that clearly defines the rights, obligations, and expectations of both parties while providing appropriate protections, remedies, and procedures for addressing disputes or performance issues. The agreement should serve as a reliable framework for a successful long-term relationship between the issuer and transfer agent, facilitating efficient shareholder administration while protecting both parties' legitimate interests.
